_note: UK market DEMO data (verified:false) — verify against real labels before indexing. No 'test' object = page shows 'label-based review, hands-on test pending' (we have not tested these with Bekon yet — honesty first).

foods.name: Eden Original Cuisine 80/20
foods.verdict: A high-meat (80/20) British recipe with fully named animal sources and no legumes high in the ingredient list. Protein at 41% dry matter is among the highest of any UK kibble. Premium price — but the cost per 1,000 kcal is moderated by high energy density.
pros: 41% protein DM — far above FEDIAF/AAFCO minimums
No legumes in the top of the ingredient list
cons: Very high protein is unnecessary (not harmful) for low-activity dogs

faq.a: Yes — it is formulated for all life stages. For large-breed puppies, check the calcium level against the 1.8% dry-matter cap before choosing any all-life-stages food.

foods.name: AATU 80/20 Chicken
foods.verdict: Single-protein, 80/20 recipe built on named free-run chicken with a long list of botanicals. Strong choice for elimination-style feeding when chicken is tolerated. One of the priciest dry foods on the UK market per 1,000 kcal.
pros: Single animal protein — useful for sensitive dogs (if chicken-tolerant)
cons: Among the most expensive UK kibbles per 1,000 kcal
Contains chicken — excluded for the most common protein allergy

faq.q: Is AATU good for dogs with allergies?
faq.a: It is single-protein, which helps elimination feeding — but chicken is the most common canine food allergen. For suspected chicken allergy choose a novel-protein or hydrolysed diet instead.

foods.name: Canagan Free-Run Chicken
foods.verdict: A well-executed grain-free British recipe with named chicken as the lead ingredient. Note: peas sit high in the ingredient list — relevant to the FDA's (unresolved) DCM context, so predisposed breeds should discuss it with a vet.
cons: Peas high in the ingredient list (DCM context — see Knowledge)
Contains chicken — excluded for chicken-allergic dogs

faq.q: Is grain-free food like Canagan safe for my dog's heart?
faq.a: The FDA never confirmed causality between grain-free diets and DCM, but legume-heavy recipes remain under discussion. For Dobermanns, Great Danes and Golden Retrievers we suggest discussing diet choice with your vet.

foods.name: Butternut Box — Chicken You Can Count On
foods.verdict: Gently cooked fresh food delivered on subscription, with named ingredients you can see in the tray. Digestibility of fresh diets is well-documented; the trade-off is cost per 1,000 kcal several times that of a good kibble.
pros: High palatability — useful for fussy eaters
cons: Cost per 1,000 kcal several times higher than kibble

faq.q: Is fresh food like Butternut Box better than kibble?
faq.a: Fresh diets show higher digestibility in studies (mostly industry-funded), but 'better health outcomes' remain unproven. Nutritionally complete kibble and fresh food can both feed a dog well — the honest difference is palatability versus price.

foods.name: Lily's Kitchen Chicken & Duck
foods.verdict: A natural-positioned recipe with named fresh chicken and duck, now under the Purina umbrella (which strengthens the manufacturer pillar: QC and nutritional staff). Protein is moderate rather than high; botanicals are marketing more than evidence.
pros: Named meat sources, no meat meal ambiguity
cons: Moderate protein for the price point

faq.q: Did Lily's Kitchen change after the Purina acquisition?
faq.a: Recipes remained branded the same; manufacturing and quality-control infrastructure is now part of Nestlé Purina. In our algorithm that improves pillar C (manufacturer credibility) — recipe scoring is unchanged.

foods.name: Burns Original Chicken & Brown Rice
foods.verdict: A deliberately simple, low-fat recipe from a vet-founded British brand — one of the few mainstream foods genuinely suitable after pancreatitis (fat 8.4% DM). Protein sits at the FEDIAF minimum: adequate for low-activity adults, not a high-performance diet.
pros: Low fat (8.4% DM) — suitable after pancreatitis
Vet-founded brand with feeding support line
cons: Rice-heavy recipe — meat is not the first ingredient

faq.q: Is Burns good for a dog with a sensitive stomach?
faq.a: Its low fat and simple recipe make it one of the safer mainstream choices for digestive issues, including recovery after pancreatitis. For diagnosed conditions, confirm with your vet — therapeutic diets may be more appropriate.

foods.name: Harringtons Complete Chicken & Rice
foods.verdict: The UK's budget-natural benchmark. Meat meal is named (chicken meal — fine by our methodology), but cereals dominate the recipe. At £2.40/kg it is hard to beat on cost per 1,000 kcal; just know what you are buying: an adequate food, not an excellent one.
pros: Named chicken meal (not anonymous 'meat and animal derivatives')
cons: Cereal-led recipe — meat is not the main ingredient

faq.q: Is Harringtons a good cheap dog food?
faq.a: It is one of the better budget options: named meat meal, no artificial colours, complete nutrition. Mid-shelf foods beat it on meat content — but at roughly double the cost per 1,000 kcal.
